turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I keep getting a bunch of new allocations every loop iteration when I try to build text, but I thought Rust was supposed to be fast with memory.

That is a very common frustration for beginners. The issue is that you are likely using the standard concatenation operator or push_mut methods in a loop without planning ahead, which forces the String to grow and reallocate its internal buffer every time it gets full.
Oh, so I'm getting a lot of heap allocations instead of just one? That sounds terrible for performance.

Exactly. Every time the buffer fills up and needs to grow, Rust has to allocate more memory on the heap, copy the old data over, and then free the old block. If you are doing this in a loop, you end up with many small allocations instead of one big allocation, which is much slower.
Is there a specific pattern I should follow to avoid that? Like preallocating the capacity?

Yes, you should use String::with_capacity to estimate how many characters you expect beforehand. Then you can iterate through your data and call write! directly into that preallocated buffer without triggering any reallocations.
So I just need to make sure I know the size before starting?

Not necessarily, but it helps a lot. If you are unsure of the exact count, you can use write! with a default buffer and let String handle resizing internally, though preallocating is definitely better when you have an estimate to avoid repeated heap allocations in your loop.
